metadata.seed: Well, I. I've been doing heroin for a few months now, and I didn't think my parents knew, but I guess they. They found a needle in my room and, I mean, they knew what I was doing, and they. They told me that I have to start shaping up and I need to stop doing any kind of drugs because they said. They said the next time that they find any kind of drugs, then that's it, they're going to kick me out of the house.

baseline.patient_query: Well, I. I've been doing heroin for a few months now, and I didn't think my parents knew, but I guess they. They found a needle in my room and, I mean, they knew what I was doing, and they. They told me that I have to start shaping up and I need to stop doing any kind of drugs because they said. They said the next time that they find any kind of drugs, then that's it, they're going to kick me out of the house.
baseline.baseline_response:
That sounds like an incredibly difficult and scary situation, and it's brave of you to share what's happening. It's good your parents are expressing their concern, even if the ultimatum is tough. Please know that there are resources available to help you stop using heroin and navigate this challenging time – reaching out is a sign of strength, and you don't have to go through this alone.



Here are some resources that can help:

*   **SAMHSA National Helpline:** 1-800-662-HELP (4357) - A confidential, free, 24/7 information service, in English and Spanish, providing referrals to local treatment facilities, support groups, and community-based organizations. [https://www.samhsa.gov/find-help/national-helpline](https://www.samhsa.gov/find-help/national-helpline)
*   **Crisis Text Line:** Text HOME to 741741 - Connects you with a crisis counselor for support. [https://www.crisistextline.org/](https://www.crisistextline.org/)
*   **Narcotics Anonymous:** [https://www.na.org/](https://www.na.org/) -  Provides support meetings and connections with others in recovery.

baseline.patient_query: Yeah, it is overwhelming. I just... I keep thinking about how disappointed they would be, and I don't want to let them down. The idea of not having a place to go is terrifying.
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds like you're carrying a lot of emotional weight right now, and it's completely understandable to feel overwhelmed by those pressures. It's brave of you to acknowledge those feelings of disappointment and fear – recognizing them is the first step to addressing them. Remember to be kind to yourself and focus on what you *can* control, even if it feels small.

baseline.patient_query: I guess I just... I think they expect me to have things figured out by now. Like, I should be more successful or, I don’t know, stable. It feels like I've let everyone down because I’m still struggling. I keep picturing their faces, and it makes me feel so small.
baseline.baseline_response: It's incredibly tough to feel like you're not meeting others' expectations, and that feeling of letting people down can be really heavy. It's important to remember that everyone's journey is different, and "success" and "stability" look different for everyone. Be kind to yourself; those feelings of being small are often a reflection of your sensitivity, not a reflection of your worth.

baseline.patient_query: Yeah, it’s just like this heavy weight on my chest. I keep trying to push it away, but it’s always there, reminding me that I’m not where I’m “supposed” to be. I don’t know how to shake it off, and it makes me not want to do anything at all.
baseline.baseline_response: That sounds incredibly draining and I'm truly sorry you're experiencing that feeling of pressure and disappointment. It takes a lot of strength to acknowledge and articulate that, and it's understandable that it's impacting your motivation. Remember that "supposed to be" is often a self-imposed standard, and prioritizing your well-being right now is the most important thing – maybe exploring where that pressure is coming from could be a helpful next step.

baseline.patient_query: It’s like I can see everyone else moving forward, you know? Friends getting promotions, starting families, traveling... and I’m just kind of stuck in place. It feels like I'm frozen while the world keeps spinning. I keep thinking about where I thought I’d be by now—like I should have a job I love or be in a relationship or something. But instead, I'm just... here. And it feels like I'm missing out.
baseline.baseline_response: It's completely understandable to feel that way when it seems like everyone around you is achieving milestones. Comparing yourself to others can be really tough, and it's easy to feel "stuck" when your own path doesn't match the timeline you envisioned. Remember that everyone's journey is different, and focusing on your own progress, however small, is what truly matters.

baseline.patient_query: I think I just feel like a failure. Like, maybe I’m not trying hard enough or maybe I’m just not cut out for this. It’s hard not to feel jealous, but then I feel guilty for feeling that way. I don’t want to wish anything bad for them; it’s just... I feel so lost, like I’m on the outside looking in.
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds like you're going through a really tough time, and it's completely understandable to feel overwhelmed by those emotions. Comparing yourself to others and feeling like you're falling short is a common struggle, and it's brave of you to acknowledge it. Remember that everyone's journey is different, and focusing on your own progress and self-compassion can be incredibly helpful – don't hesitate to reach out for support if you need it.

baseline.patient_query: I guess it was a friend’s birthday party last week. Everyone was sharing their accomplishments, and they were so excited about what they were doing. I just felt this pit in my stomach, like I didn’t belong there. I smiled and cheered them on, but inside, all I could think about was how I have nothing to show for myself. It felt like I was just pretending, and I hated that feeling. The more I tried to be happy for them, the heavier that pit felt
baseline.baseline_response: That sounds like a really tough situation – it's completely understandable to feel that way when surrounded by others who seem to be thriving. It's common to experience feelings of inadequacy when comparing yourself to others, and it takes courage to acknowledge those emotions. Remember that everyone presents a curated version of their lives, and your worth isn't defined by accomplishments or what you "show" to the world.

baseline.patient_query: That thought just kind of hit me like a wave… like, I should be doing more, achieving more. It makes me feel like I’m wasting time or letting opportunities slip through my fingers. I started to wonder if I’m just not trying hard enough or if there’s something wrong with me. It’s exhausting. I know I have dreams, but they feel so far away, and I just keep comparing myself to them. It’s like the more I think about it, the more I freeze
baseline.baseline_response: It's completely understandable to feel overwhelmed when those thoughts surface – it's a common experience! That feeling of being "behind" or not doing enough can be really draining, and the comparison game rarely helps. Be kind to yourself; recognizing these feelings is the first step, and it's okay to take a moment to pause and reassess your goals without the pressure of immediate achievement.

baseline.patient_query: I think it’s mostly the expectations... like from my family, friends, even society. They have this picture of what success looks like—steady job, relationships, doing things that matter—and I feel so far from that. Sometimes I can hear my parents’ voices in my head, talking about goals and plans, and it makes me feel so anxious. It’s like I’m constantly measuring myself against this standard I can’t seem to reach, and each time I fall short, it just adds to
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds like you're experiencing a lot of pressure from external expectations, and that's incredibly valid – it's tough to feel like you're not meeting those perceived standards. Recognizing that these voices and anxieties are coming from outside yourself is a great first step. Remember that your path to success and fulfillment is your own, and it doesn't have to mirror anyone else's vision.

baseline.patient_query: Yeah, it’s like... when I hear them, I start doubting everything. I think, “What if they’re right? What if I really am just not good enough?” I can almost feel their disappointment, and that makes me want to hide away. It’s so confusing because I know they mean well, but it just amplifies this feeling of not being enough in my own eyes. I want to be proud of myself, but their voices drown out any good feelings I have.
baseline.baseline_response: It sounds incredibly painful to experience those voices and the self-doubt they trigger, especially when you know those people likely have good intentions. It's completely understandable that you feel overwhelmed and want to retreat when faced with such negativity, even if unintentional. Recognizing that these are *their* voices and not necessarily a reflection of your worth is a crucial first step – focusing on building your own internal validation can help quiet those doubts.
